title: mathmodels - A Powerful Toolkit for Mathematical Modeling
text: Welcome mathmodels package! comprehensive user-friendly R toolkit designed practitioners researchers field mathematical modeling. core, mathmodels embodies philosophy “simplifying complex,” encapsulating sophisticated mathematical algorithms intuitive functions. allows focus model construction interpretation, rather getting bogged implementation details. mathmodels package developed companion book “Mathematical Modeling: Algorithms Programming Implementation” (China Machine Press). current version (0.0.6) focuses evaluation algorithms, offering complete suite solutions data preprocessing comprehensive evaluation.

title: mathmodels - A Powerful Toolkit for Mathematical Modeling
text: Comprehensive Rigorous Algorithms: package covers wide range mainstream evaluation analysis algorithms, including Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), Entropy Weighting, CRITIC, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) weighting, Weight Combination methods, Technique Order Preference Similarity Ideal Solution (TOPSIS), Grey Relational Analysis, Rank Sum Ratio (RSR), Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation, Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA), Inequality Measures (Gini coefficient, Theil index), System Evaluation (Coupling Coordination Degree, Obstacle Degree), Grey Prediction Models. Elegant Interface, Extremely User-Friendly: Functions designed consistency, featuring standardized parameter names. Many data preprocessing steps, normalization, scaling, indicator direction adjustment, intelligently handled internally, significantly streamlining workflow. Seamless Integration Tidyverse: integrates perfectly |> pipe operations tidyverse tools like dplyr tidyr, making data manipulation batch analysis smooth natural.

what: Comprehensive Mathematical Modeling Algorithms in R
title: Comprehensive Mathematical Modeling Algorithms in R
text: R package providing versatile toolkit mathematical modeling, developed companion book Mathematical Modeling: Algorithms Programming Implementation (China Machine Press). focuses implementing rigorous algorithms user-friendly manner. Current Version (0.0.9) adds complete time series toolkit (SARIMA, ETS, GARCH, integrated transformation visualization) overhauls epidemic modeling streamlined visualization (epi_plots.R) — top differential equation models, grey prediction, Markov chain models, rich suite evaluation methods (AHP, Entropy, CRITIC, PCA, TOPSIS, Fuzzy, RSR, DEA).

title: Comprehensive Mathematical Modeling Algorithms in R
text: Evaluation Models — AHP, Entropy weighting, CRITIC, PCA weighting, TOPSIS, Grey Relational Analysis (GRA), Rank Sum Ratio (RSR), Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation (FCE), Data Envelopment Analysis (CCR/BCC/SBM, Malmquist), plus inequality measures (Gini, Theil Index), coupling coordination degree, obstacle degree models. Prediction Models — Grey prediction (GM(1,1), GM(1,N), Verhulst), Markov chain prediction (markov_chain(), GM11_markov()), full time series toolkit: ts_transform() / ts_back_transform() Box-Cox–model–forecast–back-transform workflows; ts_ets(), ts_sarima(), ts_garch(), ts_sarima_garch() modeling; ts_stl() decomposition; ts_test() stationarity/ARCH tests; ts_forecast() unified forecasting; plus dedicated plot_ts_*() visualization functions. Differential Equation Models — String-formula ode_solver() arbitrary ODE systems; ready--use population models (Malthus, Logistic), epidemic compartment models (SI, SIS, SIR, SEIR), Lotka–Volterra predator–prey model, unified init + params interface. Epidemic visualization (plot_compartments(), plot_incidence(), plot_phase_si(), plot_Rt_estimate()) metrics (epi_metrics() R0, peak, attack rate). Tidyverse Integration — Seamlessly works |> dplyr/ggplot2 smooth data manipulation batch processing.

title: Comprehensive Mathematical Modeling Algorithms in R
code: library(mathmodels)  # --- AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) --- A = matrix(c(1,   1/2, 4, 3,   3,              2,   1,   7, 5,   5,              1/4, 1/7, 1, 1/2, 1/3,              1/3, 1/5, 2, 1,   1,              1/3, 1/5, 3, 1,   1), byrow = TRUE, nrow = 5) AHP(A)  # --- Epidemic compartment modeling --- library(ggplot2)  result = model_sir(   init   = c(S = 990, I = 10, R = 0),   params = c(beta = 0.3, gamma = 0.1),   times  = seq(0, 100, by = 0.5) )  # Visualize plot_compartments(result, compartments = c("S", "I", "R"))  # Compute epidemic metrics metrics = epi_metrics(result, beta = 0.3, gamma = 0.1, N = 1000) metrics$R0         # basic reproduction number metrics$peak_time  # time of peak infection

title: Combine Multiple Prediction Results — combine_preds
text: Combines multiple prediction results (e.g., grey prediction, time series, machine learning models) single prediction using similarity-based weighting approach, improving prediction accuracy.

title: Combine Multiple Prediction Results — combine_preds
text: x Numeric vector, prediction results combined (length >= 2).

title: Combine Multiple Prediction Results — combine_preds
text: list two elements: : Numeric, combined prediction value. w: Numeric vector, weights prediction x, summing 1.

title: Combine Multiple Prediction Results — combine_preds
text: function combines prediction results constructing similarity matrix based cosine transformation pairwise differences. Weights derived principal eigenvector similarity matrix, ensuring predictions closer higher influence. two predictions, equal weights (0.5, 0.5) used. predictions identical, equal weights assigned. Compatible mathmodels package enhancing prediction models, including grey prediction, time series, ensemble machine learning.

title: Combine Multiple Prediction Results — combine_preds
code: # Example: Combine three prediction results preds = c(100, 102, 98)  # E.g., from grey prediction, ARIMA, or ML models combine_preds(preds) #> $a #> [1] 100 #>  #> $w #> [1] 0.4142136 0.2928932 0.2928932 #>

what: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
title: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
text: Combines subjective objective weights using linear, multiplicative, game theory-based methods (geometric mean linear system).

title: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
text: w_subj Numeric vector subjective weights. w_obj Numeric vector objective weights. type Character string specifying combination method: "linear", "multiplicative", "game", "game_linear". alpha Numeric value 0 1, used linear method weight subjective weights. Defaults 0.5.

title: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
text: numeric vector combined weights, normalized sum 1.

title: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
text: function supports four methods: Linear: Combines weights alpha * w_subj + (1 - alpha) * w_obj. Multiplicative: Combines weights w_subj * w_obj, requiring positive weights. Game: Uses geometric mean (sqrt(w_subj * w_obj)) balance weights. Game_linear: Uses game-theoretic approach solving linear system based cross-product weights.

title: Combine Subjective and Objective Weights — combine_weights
code: w_subj = c(0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1) w_obj = c(0.25, 0.2, 0.3, 0.25) combine_weights(w_subj, w_obj, type = "linear", alpha = 0.6) #> [1] 0.34 0.26 0.24 0.16 combine_weights(w_subj, w_obj, type = "multiplicative") #> [1] 0.4081633 0.2448980 0.2448980 0.1020408 combine_weights(w_subj, w_obj, type = "game") #> [1] 0.3279556 0.2540333 0.2540333 0.1639778 combine_weights(w_subj, w_obj, type = "game_linear") #> [1] 0.3735683 0.2823789 0.2176211 0.1264317

title: Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation — fuzzy_eval
text: Performs fuzzy comprehensive evaluation using different fuzzy composition operators combine factor weights fuzzy evaluation matrix. Suitable multi-criteria decision analysis weights methods like AHP, entropy, CRITIC, CV, PCA.

title: Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation — fuzzy_eval
text: w Numeric vector, factor weights (e.g., combine_weights). R Numeric matrix, fuzzy evaluation matrix columns factors rows evaluation grades. Values [0, 1]. type Integer character (1-5), specifying fuzzy composition operator: 1: Min-max (main factor decisive). 2: Product-max (main factor prominent). 3: Weighted sum (additive average). 4: Bounded sum mins (min-sum bounded). 5: Normalized min-sum (balanced average).

title: Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation — fuzzy_eval
text: numeric vector normalized comprehensive evaluation results, summing 1.

title: Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation — fuzzy_eval
text: function computes fuzzy comprehensive evaluation vector B based weight vector w fuzzy evaluation matrix R. Five composition operators supported: Type 1 (min-max): max(min(w, R[j,])), emphasizes main factor. Type 2 (product-max): max(w * R[j,]), highlights main factor. Type 3 (weighted sum): sum(w * R[j,]), additive average. Type 4 (bounded sum): min(1, sum(min(w, R[j,]))), bounds sum mins. Type 5 (normalized min-sum): sum(min(w, R[j,]/sum(R[j,]))), balanced average. output B normalized sum 1. sum zero, error thrown. Uses base R lightweight implementation.

title: Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation — fuzzy_eval
code: w = c(0.3, 0.3, 0.3, 0.1)  # weights (e.g., from AHP or entropy)  # fuzzy evaluation matrix (3 grades for 4 factors) R = matrix(c(0.8, 0.7, 0.6, 0.7,              0.1, 0.2, 0.2, 0.1,              0.1, 0.1, 0.2, 0.2), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE) # Apply fuzzy comprehensive evaluation fuzzy_eval(w, R, type = 3)  # Weighted sum #> [1] 0.70 0.16 0.14

title: Grey Prediction Models — grey_models
text: GM11: List fitted values (fitted), next prediction (pnext), prediction function (f), matrix (mat), parameters (u), level ratios (lambda), range (rng). GM1N: List fitted values (fitted), posterior variance ratio (C), small error probability (P), prediction function (f). DGM21, verhulst: List fitted values (fitted), next prediction (pnext), prediction function (f), matrix (mat), parameters (u).

title: Markov Chain and Grey-Markov Prediction Models — markov
text: markov_chain constructs transition probability matrix state sequence performs multi-step prediction. states never appear starting state, equal transition probabilities assigned maintain row sums 1. ultimate stationary distribution computed via eigenvalue decomposition. GM11_markov first fits GM(1,1) model original series, classifies relative errors states using specified boundaries, builds Markov chain model error states, corrects historical fitted values future predictions.

text: functions support evaluation models mathematical modeling: tri_mf: Triangular membership, linear rise b (peak) fall c. trap_mf: Trapezoidal membership, linear rise b, plateau b c, fall d. gauss_mf: Gaussian membership, bell-shaped curve centered c spread sigma. gbell_mf: Generalized bell membership, bell-shaped curve width , shape b, center c. gauss2mf: Two-parameter Gaussian membership, combining two Gaussians spreads s1, s2 centers c1, c2. sigmoid_mf: Sigmoid membership, S-shaped curve slope inflection point b. dsigmoid_mf: Difference two sigmoids, combining slopes a1, a2 inflection points c1, c2. psigmoid_mf: Product two sigmoids, combining slopes a1, a2 inflection points c1, c2. z_mf: Z-shaped membership, decreasing 1 0 b. pi_mf: PI-shaped membership, rising b, plateau b c, falling d. s_mf: S-shaped membership, increasing 0 1 b. plot_mf: Plots membership function xlim using ggplot2, suitable tidyverse workflows. Membership values can used construct fuzzy evaluation matrices fuzzy_eval. Implemented base R, except plot_mf, requires ggplot2.

what: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
title: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
text: collection functions preprocess numeric data, including standardization, L2 norm normalization, Min-Max scaling, centered-type normalization, interval-type normalization, extreme-value-based normalization, initial-value-based normalization, mean-based normalization, negative--positive transformation. functions transform numeric vector standardized normalized scale, suitable various indicator types (positive, negative, centered, interval-based, extreme-based).

title: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
code: standardize(x, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)  normalize(x)  rescale(x, type = "+", a = 0, b = 1)  rescale_middle(x, m)  rescale_interval(x, a, b)  rescale_extreme(x, type = "+")  rescale_initial(x, type = "+")  rescale_mean(x)  to_positive(x, type = "minmax")

title: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
text: x Numeric vector preprocessed. center Logical numeric scalar, passed base::scale centering (standardize). Default TRUE. scale Logical numeric scalar, passed base::scale scaling (standardize). Default TRUE. type Character scalar specifying transformation direction method: "+" Positive direction (larger values better, rescale, rescale_extreme rescale_initial). "-" Negative direction (smaller values better, rescale rescale_extreme rescale_initial). "minmax" Min-max transformation (to_positive). "reciprocal" Reciprocal transformation (to_positive). Numeric scalar, lower bound output range optimal interval (rescale rescale_interval). b Numeric scalar, upper bound output range optimal interval (rescale rescale_interval). m Numeric scalar, optimal value centered-type normalization (rescale_middle).

title: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
text: numeric vector length x, transformed follows: standardize: Standardized values (mean = 0, sd = 1). normalize: L2 norm normalized values (Euclidean norm, unit length). rescale: Min-Max scaled values [, b] (default [0, 1]). rescale_middle: Centered-type normalized values [0, 1], 1 indicates x = m. rescale_interval: Interval-type normalized values [0, 1], 1 indicates x [, b]. rescale_extreme: Extreme-based normalized values using min(x)/x (positive) x/max(x) (negative). rescale_initial: Initial-based normalized values using x/x[1] x[1]/x. rescale_mean: Mean-based normalized values using x/mean(x). to_positive: Transformed values converting negative indicators positive using min-max reciprocal transformation.

title: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
text: functions support various preprocessing needs data analysis: standardize: Applies Z-score standardization (mean = 0, sd = 1), ideal equalizing variances normally distributed data. normalize: Scales vector unit length dividing L2 (Euclidean) norm, useful machine learning similarity calculations. rescale: Performs Min-Max scaling specified range (default [0, 1]), supporting positive negative indicators. rescale_middle: Normalizes centered-type indicators, values closer optimal value m better, mapping [0, 1]. rescale_interval: Normalizes interval-type indicators, values within [, b] optimal, mapping [0, 1]. rescale_extreme: Normalizes using extreme values: min(x)/x positive indicators x/max(x) negative indicators, often used grey relational analysis. rescale_initial: Normalizes dividing first value (x/x[1] x[1]/x), commonly used grey relational analysis. rescale_mean: Normalizes dividing mean (x/mean(x)), commonly used grey relational analysis. to_positive: Converts negative indicators positive using either min-max (max(x) - x) reciprocal (1/x) transformation. Missing values (NA) preserved output. rescale_initial rescale_mean, initial value mean must non-zero, respectively.

title: Preprocessing Functions for Data Normalization and Standardization — preprocess
code: # Standardization x = c(4, 1, NA, 5, 8) standardize(x) #> [1] -0.1732051 -1.2124356         NA  0.1732051  1.2124356  # L2 norm normalization normalize(x) #> [1] 0.38851434 0.09712859         NA 0.48564293 0.77702869  # Min-Max normalization (positive direction) rescale(x)                # Scale to \code{[0, 1]} #> [1] 0.4285714 0.0000000        NA 0.5714286 1.0000000 rescale(x, type = "-", a = 0.002, b = 0.996)  # Reverse scaling #> [1] 0.570 0.996    NA 0.428 0.002  # Negative-to-positive transformation to_positive(x)                       # Min-max transformation #> [1]  4  7 NA  3  0 to_positive(x, type = "reciprocal")  # Reciprocal transformation #> [1] 0.250 1.000    NA 0.200 0.125  # Centered-type normalization PH = 6:9 rescale_middle(PH, 7) #> [1] 0.5 1.0 0.5 0.0  # Interval-type normalization Temp = c(35.2, 35.8, 36.6, 37.1, 37.8, 38.4) rescale_interval(Temp, 36, 37) #> [1] 0.4285714 0.8571429 1.0000000 0.9285714 0.4285714 0.0000000  # Extreme-based normalization rescale_extreme(x)         # min(x)/x #> [1] 0.500 0.125    NA 0.625 1.000 rescale_extreme(x, "-")    # x/max(x) #> [1] 0.250 1.000    NA 0.200 0.125  # Initial-based normalization rescale_initial(x) #> [1] 1.00 0.25   NA 1.25 2.00  # Mean-based normalization rescale_mean(x) #> [1] 0.8888889 0.2222222        NA 1.1111111 1.7777778

EG, contains region, industry, indicator columns (e.g., employment output). region name column data specifying region (used LQ EG). total name column data specifying total (used LQ). .cols columns data calculate location quotient (used LQ). .Optional grouping column LQ, defaults NULL (grouping). x numeric vector calculating HHI (used HHI). scaled Logical; TRUE, HHI scaled account number firms. Defaults FALSE. industry name column data specifying industry (used EG). y name column data specifying indicator (e.g., employment output, used EG).

title: Regional Economics Functions — regional_economics
text: LQ data frame region column calculated location quotients specified columns, optionally grouped .. HHI numeric value representing HHI, either scaled unscaled. EG tibble two columns: industry name corresponding EG index value.

title: Regional Economics Functions — regional_economics
text: LQ Calculates Location Quotient multiple columns optional grouping. LQ measures relative concentration industry region compared national benchmark. function assumes first row data contains national totals, first two columns specifying region total, remaining columns used LQ calculation. HHI Calculates Herfindahl-Hirschman Index, measure market concentration based squared sum market shares. scaled = TRUE, HHI normalized account number firms. EG Calculates Ellison-Glaeser Index, measures geographic concentration industry controlling firm size distribution random distribution effects. requires data regions, industries, indicator (e.g., employment output).

title: Regional Economics Functions — regional_economics

title: TOPSIS Method for Multi-Criteria Decision Making — topsis
text: named numeric vector relative closeness scores ([0, 1]) alternative. Higher values indicate better alternatives. Names taken rownames(X) default "Sample1", "Sample2", etc.

title: TOPSIS Method for Multi-Criteria Decision Making — topsis
text: TOPSIS method ranks alternatives : Normalizing decision matrix using L2 norm normalization. Applying weights form weighted normalized matrix. Identifying positive negative ideal solutions based indicator directions. Computing Euclidean distances ideal solutions. Calculating relative closeness S0 / (S0 + Sstar), S0 distance negative ideal Sstar distance positive ideal. implementation supports positive negative indicators via index parameter.
